channel.title: todo on Hugo
channel.description: Recent content in todo on Hugo

item.title: algorithms
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(\def	extsc#1{\dosc#1\csod} \def\dosc#1#2\csod{{m #1{\small #2}}}\) 1 study guide in order: algorithm correctness, without the recursion part asymptotic notations and time complexity selection sort, bubble sort recursion correctness of recursive algorithms

item.title: networking
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) this document is still being constructed, it should not be used for reference yet. i have yet to add citations from the syllabus of the course from syllabus.docx 1 storage units basic units table: unit description bit 1 or 0 byte 8 bits kilobyte 1024 bytes megabyte 1024 kilobytes gigabyte 1024 megabytes terabyte 1024 gigabytes petabyte 1024 terabytes 2 unit prefix table prefix exponent yocto \(10^{-24}\) zepto \(10^{-21}\) atto \(10^{-18}\) femto \(10^{-15}\) pico \(10^{-12}\) nano \(10^{-9}\) micro \(10^{-6}\) milli \(10^{-3}\) kilo \(10^3\) mega \(10^6\) giga \(10^9\) tera \(10^{12}\) peta \(10^{15}\) exa \(10^{18}\) zetta \(10^{21}\) yotta \(10^{24}\) 3 internet at home there are 4 computers and a router (therefore, 5 “stations”) connected to the same channel.

item.title: symacs
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) symacs, which stands for symbolic emacs is a latex-based interface to sympy built with emacs integration in mind symacs looks for common math writing patterns and parses them into sympy objects to then evaluate them symbolically 1 motivation for a latex-based computer algebra system interface 2 sage vs sympy sagemath is far sure much more powerful than sympy, but sympy is just a python library (about 10mb) and sagemath packs lots of software (including sympy and maxima) that amount to gigabytes of space, so i decided to just go with sympy, which hopefully will be much more powerful in the future as it has a huge community 3 parsing latex math expressions texsoup does all of the work from TexSoup import TexSoup mytex = r'$E_1=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & 1},E_2=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ 1 & 0},E_3=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & -1},E_4=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ -1 & 0}$' soup = TexSoup(mytex) print(soup.
